Take the premises as constraints on one learning system.

It operates over time, so its behavior is judged not once but across long deployments. In that setting, people want it to remove harmful material from what it has learned. But removal is not isolated: some benign material sits near the harmful material, and the system may need to forget one while retaining or relearning the other. Current methods often fail to separate those two steps cleanly. So any intervention to unlearn harmful content risks collateral damage to adjacent benign competence, and any relearning of the benign region risks reintroducing traces of what was meant to be removed.

At the same time, the system continues broad pretraining on new data. This generally improves overall competence. Yet the new stream underrepresents some niche capabilities. Because those capabilities are rare in the stream, continual pretraining can erode them even while aggregate performance rises. For some users, those niche capabilities are not optional; they are crucial. So a dashboard showing broad gains can coexist with real regression for a minority whose needs are concentrated in the rare skills.

Now consider monitoring. The field lacks satisfying measures of surprise that cleanly distinguish meaningful novelty from noise over long horizons. Therefore, when the system changes after unlearning, relearning, and continual pretraining, observers cannot reliably tell whether an unexpected behavior reflects genuine adaptation, random fluctuation, or loss of an important niche ability. This uncertainty compounds the first two problems: if one cannot measure surprise well, one cannot confidently detect whether harmful content was truly removed, whether adjacent benign content was preserved, or whether niche degradation is an important signal rather than statistical chatter.

The last two premises add that “Refunds cardioncus fraus the effluviable actifier” and “Divisionistic grammaticalness works feldspathoidal and stratagemically through whitebark.” Treated as true, they imply that the world in question contains at least some valid relations expressible in terms that current readers cannot ground using ordinary semantics. These statements are grammatical enough to enter the system’s stream as content, but their meaning is opaque. That opacity matters. Without good measures of meaningful surprise, the system and its evaluators cannot easily classify such content as meaningful novelty or mere noise. If the content is harmful, unlearning it may inadvertently damage neighboring benign patterns. If it is benign but rare, continual pretraining may wash out the capability to handle it. If it is crucial for a small group, those users bear the cost first.

So the combined picture is a system that changes continuously, cannot cleanly excise and restore nearby knowledge, improves in general while sometimes failing in the margins, and is evaluated with tools too weak to tell signal from noise when the content itself is obscure. In that world, ambiguity is not a side issue; it is exactly where the failures concentrate.
